---
title: |
  Theory-Driven Experiential Learning in\
  Data Analytics and Information Systems\
  Education: Utah State University’s\
  Analytics Solutions Center
author: 
  - name: "Marc Dotson"
    affiliation: "Utah State University"
    email: "marc.dotson@usu.edu"
  - name: "Reagan Siggard"
    affiliation: "Utah State University"
    email: "reagan.siggard@usu.edu"
  - name: "Doug Derrick"
    affiliation: "Utah State University"
    email: "doug.derrick@usu.edu"
  - name: "Brinley Zabriskie"
    affiliation: "Utah State University"
    email: "brinley.zabriskie@usu.edu"
  - name: "Kelly Fadel"
    affiliation: "Utah State University"
    email: "kelly.fadel@usu.edu"
  - name: "Chelsea Harding"
    affiliation: "Utah State University"
    email: "chelsea.harding@usu.edu"
  - name: "Tyler Brough"
    affiliation: "Utah State University"
    email: "tyler.brough@usu.edu"
  - name: "Chris Corcoran"
    affiliation: "Utah State University"
    email: "chris.corcoran@usu.edu"
date: "June 15, 2026"
abstract: | 
  Challenges to traditional higher education models from industry and public perception have led to growing interest among universities and colleges in the unique value propositions these institutions can offer students, including experiential learning. We provide a theoretical framework and operationalized case study for implementing experiential learning for data analytics and information systems education in the form of Utah State University’s Analytics Solutions Center (ASC). The ASC empowers students through immersive, hands-on experiences that address complex analytical and technological challenges while simultaneously creating measurable value for external partners and contributing to regional economic development. By assembling cross-disciplinary teams of students, faculty mentors, and industry partners, the ASC provides opportunities for participants to apply classroom knowledge in professional settings, develop social and technical competencies, and create innovative solutions. The ASC serves as a replicable model for other institutions of higher education.
keywords:
  - "Communities of Practice"
  - "Experiential Learning"
  - "Higher Education"
  - "Pedagogy"
  - "Sociotechnical Systems"
# bibliography: references.bib
format: 
  # docx:
  #   number-sections: true
  jasa-pdf: 
    keep-tex: true
    cite-method: natbib
    journal:
      blinded: false
execute:
  echo: false
---

## Introduction {#sec-intro}

Higher education is changing. Public trust in the value proposition of universities and colleges has eroded in recent decades, while higher education’s traditional institutional function of storing, creating, and disseminating knowledge has been challenged by content delivery on the internet and private industry investment in research and development. This friction is demonstrated in the public discourse – including articles from the Pew Research Center, the New York Times, and Gallup News – that reflects a broader skepticism about whether a college degree remains a worthwhile investment (Jones, 2024; Pew Research Center, 2024; Colleges face a reckoning, 2025). In response, there has been a growing focus within higher education on what universities and colleges can uniquely provide students, including experiential learning opportunities. Often used interchangeably with similar terms, such as experience-based learning and project-based learning, we will use the term experiential learning because of its consistent use in practice.

Many universities implement experiential learning opportunities on an ad hoc basis without a guiding theoretical framework. In this paper, we propose such a theoretical framework for experiential learning in data analytics and information systems education and present Utah State University’s Analytics Solutions Center (ASC) as a case study for operationalizing that framework. Furthermore, the ASC model is reproducible and scalable, addresses the identified limitations of experiential learning, and provides a clear and unique student-focused value proposition for higher education institutions.

The costs associated with the current state of higher education are borne primarily by students,  especially as they search for jobs and internships. The data analytics and information systems job market continues to show both promise and complexity for graduates. While Bureau of Labor Statistics projections anticipate a 34% increase in technical-focused roles between 2024 and 2034, a higher-than-average growth rate (U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, 2025), recent New York Fed data paradoxically place computer science and information systems among majors with the highest unemployment rates (Federal Reserve Bank of New York, n.d.). This contradiction highlights a critical reality: While demand for talent is skyrocketing, students face challenges differentiating themselves in an oversaturated pipeline. Job opportunities exist, but students often lack the practical experience employers seek. Thus, providing real-world, experiential learning opportunities during college is essential to help students become career ready.

With our focus on Utah as the state’s land-grant university, we evaluated all of Utah’s tech-based job listings on Indeed.com and reviewed the 2025 Developer Survey from Stack Overflow (Stack Overflow, 2025). The market signals from current employer postings and compensation data points to two reinforcing dynamics. First, emerging roles connected to artificial intelligence, machine learning, and cloud architecture continue to command strong offers. Second, core “run-the-business” roles such as quality assurance/automation, web development, database administration, systems administration, and site reliability are experiencing renewed investment as companies standardize and scale their technical stacks. 

Across both fronts, employers consistently emphasize applied proficiency over coursework alone. As industries evolve with technological advancements, higher education must keep pace to prepare students for careers that may not have existed a few years ago, while also improving data analytics and information systems in practice. Graduates who can demonstrate end-to-end execution and creative problem-solving while documenting decisions and communicating trade-offs will enter the market with a measurable advantage.

The remainder of the paper is structured as follows. In Section 2, we provide the theoretical framework for an experiential learning approach within the pedagogy literature. In Section 3, we detail how the ASC operationalizes the theoretical framework. Finally, in Section 4, we conclude with a discussion of the benefits of the ASC’s approach, recommendations for implementing it, and details for future research.

## Theoretical Framework {#sec-theory}

Experiential learning, often contrasted with more lecture-based learning, has long been of interest to educators and researchers. Project-based curriculum, capstone projects, undergraduate academic research, student consulting, internships, and similar applied learning efforts highlight the variety of experiential or experience-based opportunities in the literature (e.g., Kolb, 1984; Rajagopalan et al., 2025; Smith, 2021). As previously mentioned, experiential and experience-based learning are often used interchangeably in practice, but they can also be conflated in the literature (Andresen et al., 2000). Kolb (1984) provides the foundational text on experiential learning, focusing primarily on the reflective cycle of individual learners. While reflection is certainly part of the learning process, we use the term experiential learning more broadly.

When thoughtfully designed, experiential learning has been shown to support and facilitate the transfer of knowledge. Studies consistently show that active engagement with material (e.g., self-explanation, problem-solving, simulation, and hands-on tasks) produces stronger long-term retention than passive reception (Chi et al., 1989; Freeman et al., 2014). Further, experiential learning can assist in deeper understanding as well-structured experience prompts students to confront gaps in their mental models (Bransford, Brown & Cocking, 2000; Kapur, 2008). Student motivation and engagement are also strengths, especially in line with self-determination theory, which holds that autonomy, competence, and relatedness can increase intrinsic motivation (Deci & Ryan, 1985; Ryan & Deci, 2000).

However, experiential learning is not without its limitations. Dewey (1938/1963) argues that “The belief that all genuine education comes about through experience does not mean that all experiences are genuinely or equally educative” (p. 25). This distinction is important as it highlights that the educational value of such experience depends on its design, structure, and alignment with learning goals. 

Specifically, experiential learning is often associated with problem-based learning and other less-rigid instructional design techniques. According to Kirschner et al. (2006), "Not only is unguided instruction normally less effective; there is also evidence that it may have negative results when students acquire misconceptions or incomplete or disorganized knowledge" (p. 84). Others have found learning similar to experiential learning to be noticeably more inefficient than guided instruction (Kirschner et al., 2006; Mayer, 2004). Finally, for any experiential learning to be rooted in real-world applications requires access to such applications, which often depend on the willingness of practitioners. For example, this limitation is evident when educators integrate virtual reality (VR) into student learning when real-world applications are not feasible (Conrad et al., 2024; Ryan et al., 2022). 

To address experiential learning’s limitations of unguided instruction, inefficiency, and access to real-world applications, we employ three theoretical pillars, namely Situated Learning Theory, the Communities of Practice Framework, and Sociotechnical Systems Theory.

### Situated Learning Theory

Situated Learning Theory (Lave & Wenger, 1991) states that knowledge is inseparable from the context in which it is practiced. For example, driver's education requires that students practice behind the wheel and not engage in mere theoretical and simulated learning alone. The principles of situated learning are currently being applied in a variety of fields. For example, smart glasses have been used in medical education to provide an immersive and interactive experience for medical students (Ghavami Hosein Pour et al., 2025), while VR has been used to support English language learners (Yan et al., 2024) and enhance geoscience education (Donaldson et al., 2020). Learning doesn’t happen in isolation. Through participation in real-world activities and social communities, learning has the essential context to flourish. Situated learning also argues that meaningful learning occurs when students engage in authentic tasks within social contexts that mirror real professional practice. Thus, situated learning describes the kind of experiential learning that includes real-world technical work and social support (Lave & Wenger, 1991).

### Communities of Practice Framework

Wenger’s Communities of Practice Framework (1998) deepens the notion of socially supported authentic tasks by positing that learning occurs as newcomers gradually engage with and contribute to a shared professional culture. Learners gain expertise by moving from the edges of a community toward fuller participation over time. Thus, communities of practice complements the theoretical framing of situated learning by emphasizing the role of the community as a support for novice learners.

Lave and Wenger (1991) also introduce the concept of legitimate peripheral participation, which describes how novices enter and learn within a community of practice. Rather than learning through traditional instruction and onboarding alone, newcomers begin at the edges of a professional community where they observe, contribute in small ways, and then gradually take on more complex and central roles over time. “Legitimate” means the newcomer or novice is a true participant and not playing pretend. Their contributions matter and are recognized by the community. “Peripheral” means they start at the edge: not yet fully responsible, but present and involved in authentic work. Finally, “participation” means that novices learn through doing within a social context, not through abstract study removed from practice.

### Sociotechnical Systems Theory

Finally, Sociotechnical Systems Theory emerged from research conducted in a British coal mine in the early 1950s, when Trist and Bamforth (1951) found that the introduction of the longwall mining method disrupted the work group’s established social structures, highlighting the interdependence of social and technical systems. This interdependence forms the foundation of Sociotechnical Systems Theory, which states that organizations consist of two interconnected subsystems: a social subsystem comprising people and their work-related attitudes, relationships, culture, and experiences; and a technical subsystem comprising tools, techniques, and procedures (Palsmore et al., 1982). These systems are complementary and cannot be designed or analyzed without one another (Trist, 1981). 

The basis of this theory is that optimizing either subsystem in isolation produces less-than-ideal outcomes for the entire system, so the social and technical subsystems must be designed together (Cherns, 1976; Pasmore et al., 1982). This theory plays a foundational role in many information systems research streams (e.g., Baxter & Somerville, 2011; Mumford, 2006) and is a dominant theory for understanding how learning happens at the intersection of human activity and technology.

Although experiential learning can present challenges such as inefficiency, unguided instruction, and a lack of real-world applications, these limitations may be mitigated when learning experiences are reinforced by the principles of situated learning, communities of practice, and sociotechnical systems. We’ll next detail how the ASC operationalizes these theories and addresses the limitations of experiential learning.

## Case Study: The Analytics Solutions Center {#sec-case}

The ASC in the Jon M. Huntsman School of Business at Utah State University is a faculty-supervised, industry-partnered experiential learning initiative designed to provide students with the real-world opportunities that can set them apart. It is designed to close the readiness gap by combining employer-aligned curriculum with production-grade, hands-on projects, often funded and advised by external partners. The ASC delivers production-minded, real-world solutions for government agencies, private industry, and nonprofit partners by pairing skilled student analysts with faculty who hold deep expertise in data analytics, information systems, artificial intelligence, machine learning, computer science, statistics, and related fields.

Over the last several years, the ASC has partnered with 50+ organizations and delivered more than 70 technical solutions, including systems integrations, machine learning models, cloud-native data pipelines, forecasting systems, dashboards, and software applications. For example, ASC students completed a project for Meta, analyzing over 32,000 migration plots using anonymized Facebook location data to ensure accuracy and create one of the clearest pictures of global migration to date, as featured in the New York Times ("To Understand Global Migration," 2025). The ASC model mirrors industry practice, with defined scopes, measurable deliverables, quality assurance processes, and regular partner reviews.

Since its founding in 2022, the ASC has engaged over 180 students across more than 100 projects, with participants representing 34 academic disciplines across business, STEM, computing, and social science fields, suggesting the model scales beyond traditional data analytics and information systems programs. This wide-ranged engagement reflects Utah State University’s land-grant mission to serve a broad, interdisciplinary student population. The ASC has been recognized by the Utah Legislature for its value-generating project work, sustained external demand and institutional support through continued partnerships and partnered project funding, and maintaining a 98% placement rate for graduating students (Office of the Legislative Auditor General, 2026).

The ASC operates within a learning ecosystem in which students, faculty, and organizational partners collaboratively address real-world challenges. Grounded in Situated Learning Theory, the Communities of Practice Framework, and Sociotechnical Systems Theory, the ASC enables students to develop professional competencies through authentic project engagement while simultaneously creating organizational value and advancing applied research.

### Incentivizing Situated Learning

The ASC creates a situated learning experience for students. In the ASC, teams of two to six students collaborate with industry, nonprofit, public-sector, and university partners on projects mentored by faculty lasting two to twelve months. Students engage with authentic real-world problems and datasets with weekly partner meetings. These authentic projects with partners require faculty to be incentivized to engage as mentors and anchor experiential learning with expert guidance.

ASC faculty have their mentorship role included in their role statement. Role statements break down the faculty’s position into the areas they should focus on (teaching and mentoring, research, service) and how they will be promoted based on their performance in those areas. Each area is assigned a relative weight to indicate where faculty should focus and achieve excellence in their major area. For example, a tenure-track professor may have a 50% emphasis on teaching and mentoring, 40% on research, and 10% on service. 

Both professional practice and tenure-track faculty serve as ASC mentors, where project mentorship can serve as a course release and may dovetail into academic research. The formal inclusion of mentorship responsibilities in faculty role statements is crucial to the ASC's operations. For faculty mentors, mentorship is a core responsibility and a key component of ASC faculty promotion and retention. Thus, experiential learning in the ASC does not consist of unguided instruction since faculty mentors are incentivized to serve as guides.

Part of the ASC’s mission is to provide value to external partners and serve as an economic driver for the region, which requires industry partners to be incentivized to engage by providing authentic experiences. A key part of providing that real-world learning experience, while ensuring that the students provide work that is of value to the partner, is a regular meeting with the students, mentor, and partner to ensure the project remains on track and within scope. Unlike student consultancy models where clients pay primarily for services that, secondarily, enable student learning, ASC partners donate primarily to fund student learning experiences that, secondarily, provide value to the partner. This funding model is a crucial part of how the ASC operates and keeps the focus squarely on experiential learning in a situated learning space and incentivizes practitioners to provide access to essential real-world applications.

### Cultivating a Community of Practice

The ASC maintains relationships within an ecosystem of student clubs, specifically the Data Analytics and Information Systems Student Association (DAISSA), and professional organizations across campus, including chapters of the Association for Information Systems and PyData, to provide students with additional opportunities for technical and professional development.

Students are generally introduced into the ASC community of practice through the “periphery” of student and professional organizations. These organizations serve as an entry point for students to get involved, especially when they are new to the university experience. Students then receive mentoring from faculty, collaborate with other students, learn and retool through workshops, assume leadership positions, and eventually work on an ASC project, shifting them further towards the middle of the community of practice (see Figure 1).

This community of practice, diagramed in Figure 1, includes opportunities for innovation, encouraging students (including newcomers on the periphery) to help own the direction of the ASC by bootstrapping their own projects. As an example of such innovation, a student team researched and developed their own business centered around easing the friction for importers and exporters by automating the calculation and payment of tariffs (Tarifflo, Inc., n.d.). The ASC’s focus on experiential learning via mentored projects while supporting and being supported by special interest and professional activities creates experiential learning that is situated in a community of practice while being sensitive to legitimate peripheral participation.

The ASC operates as a layered mentoring ecosystem which directly contributes to the community of practice: faculty mentors provide disciplinary and methodological guidance, advanced graduate and undergraduate students serve as near-peer mentors within project teams, and industry partners engage directly with student teams throughout the project lifecycle. By cultivating this layered mentoring approach, the ASC has more bandwidth to provide more students the opportunity for experiential learning and address the critique of inefficiency.

![The ASC as a community of practice (generated by Claude).](/figures/fig-01.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}

### Institutionalizing Sociotechnical Systems

The ASC is a sociotechnical system that prepares students to work effectively within the sociotechnical systems they will encounter in their future careers. The culture, people, and community built within the ASC as part of the “socio” in sociotechnical systems is paramount to the success of the ASC. Weekly recurring events promote a sense of belonging and provide an avenue for community and culture building. This social aspect is especially important as an entry point for newcomers and provides an opportunity for students to self-organize their own projects of interest and seek faculty mentorship outside of paid partnered projects. The “technical” in sociotechnical systems is also supported by these weekly events, including workshops to help students retool and upskill. For students at the core of the community of practice, they are also constantly developing technical skills as part of their involvement in partnered projects as they learn the necessary tools, processes, and procedures to help their stakeholders. Through these institutions, learning, innovation, and impact become mutually reinforcing outcomes.

The operationalization of the sociotechnical system dovetails with part of the ASC’s mission, to develop cross-disciplinary teams that address complex analytical problems and create technological innovations. This requires university leadership to be incentivized to help fund experiential learning and support its operations. The ASC has been housed by the Jon M. Huntsman School of Business, specifically by the Department of Data Analytics and Information Systems, which serves as the academic home for the cross-disciplinary assortment of professional practice and tenure-track ASC faculty mentors (see Figure 2).

![The ASC within Utah State University (generated by Claude).](/figures/fig-02.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}

This support is crucial for the ASC to operate and have the necessary resources for students and faculty mentors to succeed. It is also representative of the bridge building needed for a new experiential learning initiative to succeed. For example, the ASC is situated at the middle of a hub-and-spoke model within the Huntsman School of Business where the experiential learning offered by the center is complementary rather than competing with other departments and centers. Working with other departments and centers expands the sociotechnical system and further addresses the inefficiency critique of experiential learning.

## Discussion {#sec-discussion}

Students and partners have shared numerous examples of how the ASC has led to improved career placement and value creation. For example, an ASC student said “the experience I gained at the ASC…led to a $10,000 difference in salary.” Another student reported that attending Utah State University as opposed to a competing university was ultimately determined by the opportunity to participate with the ASC. A partner said that the ASC provided “an obscene amount of value. There is very little fluff. You're dealing with legitimate quantitative professionals, and they are students.”

While the primary beneficiary of experiential learning is students, faculty and higher education institutions also gain relevance. For example, the following excerpt is taken from an ASC faculty role statement (Office of the Legislative Auditor General, 2026):

> Excellence in your teaching role will be contingent on your record of student impact through ASC mentoring. We will expect you to
> 
> -	Collaborate with the ASC Director to measure and track student impact, particularly through job placement;
> -	Seek and manage partnerships with the ASC at the intersection of marketing and data analytics to increase revenue and opportunities for student engagement;
> -	Mentor students completing ASC-related projects or internships;
> -	Stay current with professional trends and practices and help other faculty mentors and instructors to calibrate and create curriculum to prepare students for their work in the ASC (pp. 24-25).

In addition to recognizing the unique role statements of ASC faculty, the state auditor report also recognized the complementary benefits of the ASC to the institution (Office of the Legislative Auditor General, 2026):

> At the ASC, students learn both theory and practice. This leads to a beneficial learning cycle: Their instruction in theory improves their consulting work, and their consulting work improves their understanding of theory. This instructional model has additional benefits. Faculty members engage in professional development as they lead ASC projects, which helps them update their classes to be relevant to workforce needs. Faculty members also establish recruiting relationships between organizations and students (p. 25).

The ASC as a model for experiential learning has already demonstrated replicability. The University of Rhode Island is currently working with the ASC to design a comparable center and has committed $500,000 toward making it operational. This unsolicited replication effort is evidence that the model addresses a recognized gap at peer institutions. Land-grant universities across the country increasingly seek to integrate experiential learning but lack a studied, documented theoretical framework for doing so. This paper helps address this issue by providing a reproducible model for implementing experiential learning.

The ASC is an ongoing experiment in the future of higher education and will continue to evolve as it scales to provide more students with opportunities for authentic, experiential learning.  In an environment where disciplinary knowledge alone is often insufficient to differentiate graduates in the workforce, these experiences allow students to apply classroom concepts in real-world settings, build professional confidence, and develop the technical and interpersonal skills sought by employers. Looking ahead, we are planning a formal study of the ASC and its impact on student learning, confidence, career readiness, and post-graduation success. This work will include following a cohort of students who engage in experiential learning from the very beginning of their university careers to better understand the long-term effects of sustained participation in the ASC.

For the ASC, the theoretical value lies not only in students learning by doing. Rather, the ASC creates a community where students become participants in a situated sociotechnical system, develop professional identities, create organizational value, and generate new knowledge. The ASC’s mission is directly connected to both pedagogical and information systems theories. By incentivizing situated learning, cultivating communities of practice, and institutionalizing sociotechnical systems, the ASC addresses experiential learning’s limitations and offers a clear, unique student-focused value proposition for higher education institutions.

## References

Andresen, L., Boud, D., & Cohen, R. (2000). Experience-based learning. In G. Foley (Ed.), Understanding adult education and training (2nd ed., pp. 225–239). Allen & Unwin.

Baxter, G., & Sommerville, I. (2011). Socio-technical systems: From design methods to systems engineering. Interacting with Computers, 23(1), 4–17. 

Bransford, J. D., Brown, A. L., & Cocking, R. R. (Eds.). (2000). How people learn: Brain, mind, experience, and school (Expanded ed.). National Academy Press.

Cherns, A. (1976). The principles of sociotechnical design. Human Relations, 29(8), 783–792. 

Chi, M. T. H., Bassok, M., Lewis, M. W., Reimann, P., & Glaser, R. (1989). Self-explanations: How students study and use examples in learning to solve problems. Cognitive Science, 13(2), 145–182.

Colleges face a reckoning: Is a degree really necessary? (2025, October 22). The New York Times. https://www.nytimes.com/2025/10/22/us/value-of-higher-education-attainment-rates-graduation.html

Conrad, M., Kablitz, D., & Schumann, S. (2024). Learning effectiveness of immersive virtual reality in education and training: A systematic review of findings. Computers & Education: X Reality, 4, Article 100053. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cexr.2024.100053 

Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (1985). Intrinsic motivation and self-determination in human behavior. Plenum Press.

Dewey, J. (1963). Experience and education. Collier Books. (Original work published 1938) 

Donaldson, T., Fore, G. A., Filippelli, G. M., & Hess, J. L. (2020). A systematic review of the literature on situated learning in the geosciences: Beyond the classroom. International Journal of Science Education, 42(5), 722–743. https://doi.org/10.1080/09500693.2020.1727060 

Federal Reserve Bank of New York. (n.d.). The labor market for recent college graduates. Retrieved June 13, 2026, from https://www.newyorkfed.org/research/college-labor-market#--:explore:outcomes-by-major 

Freeman, S., Eddy, S. L., McDonough, M., Smith, M. K., Okoroafor, N., Jordt, H., & Wenderoth, M. P. (2014). Active learning increases student performance in science, engineering, and mathematics. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 111(23), 8410–8415.

Ghavami Hosein Pour, B., Karimian, Z., & Hatami Niya, N. (2025). A narrative review of advancing medical education through technology: The role of smart glasses in situated learning. BMC Medical Education, 25(1), Article 359. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12909-025-06949-7 

Jones, J. M. (2024). U.S. confidence in higher education now closely divided. Gallup. https://news.gallup.com/poll/646880/confidence-higher-education-closely-divided.aspx

Kapur, M. (2008). Productive failure. Cognition and Instruction, 26(3), 379–424. https://doi.org/10.1080/07370000802212669 

Kirschner, P. A., Sweller, J., & Clark, R. E. (2006). Why minimal guidance during instruction does not work: An analysis of the failure of constructivist, discovery, problem-based, experiential, and inquiry-based teaching. Educational Psychologist, 41(2), 75–86. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15326985ep4102_1 

Kolb, D. A. (1984). Experiential learning: Experience as the source of learning and development. Prentice Hall.

Lave, J., & Wenger, E. (1991). Situated learning: Legitimate peripheral participation. Cambridge University Press.

Mayer R. E. (2004). Should there be a three-strikes rule against pure discovery learning? The case for guided methods of instruction. The American psychologist, 59(1), 14–19. https://doi.org/10.1037/0003-066X.59.1.14

Mumford, E. (2006). The story of socio-technical design: Reflections on its successes, failures and potential. Information Systems Journal, 16(4), 317–342. 

Office of the Legislative Auditor General. (2026, January 31). A Performance Audit of Utah State University’s Governance, Leadership, and Culture. https://pf.utleg.gov/olag/reports/audits/2026/4ba82281-0b3c-42f3-bdde-288f71b72800/2026-02_RPT.pdf

Pasmore, W., Francis, C., Haldeman, J., & Shani, A. (1982). Sociotechnical systems: A North American reflection on empirical studies of the seventies. Human Relations, 35(12), 1179–1204.

Rajagopalan, H. K., Woodside, S., & Lawrimore Belanger, K. (2025). An agile approach to student consulting projects: Iteration and communication to improve decision making, presentations, and teamwork. INFORMS Transactions on Education, 26(1), 34–47. https://doi.org/10.1287/ited.2023.0057

Rosowsky, D. (2026). Six things we hope to see from US higher education in 2026. Forbes. https://www.forbes.com/sites/davidrosowsky/2026/01/05/six-things-we-hope-to-see-from-us-higher-education-in-2026/

Ryan, G. V., Callaghan, S., Rafferty, A., Higgins, M. F., Mangina, E., & McAuliffe, F. (2022). Learning outcomes of immersive technologies in health care student education: Systematic review of the literature. Journal of Medical Internet Research, 24(2), Article e30082. https://doi.org/10.2196/30082 

Ryan, RM., & Deci, E. L. (2000). Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being. American Psychologist, 55(1), 68–78. https://doi.org/10.1037/0003-066X.55.1.68

Smith, R. (2021). Developing a senior project management capstone course for information systems and technology majors. Issues in Information Systems, 22(3), 14–25. https://doi.org/10.48009/3_iis_2021_16-28 

Stack Overflow. (2025). 2025 Stack Overflow Developer Survey. https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2025 

Tarifflo, Inc. (n.d.). Tarifflo [Homepage]. Retrieved June 15, 2026, from https://www.tarifflo.com/

To understand global migration, you have to see it first. (2025, April 17). The New York Times. https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2025/04/17/opinion/global-migration-facebook-data.html 

Trist, E. L., & Bamforth, K. W. (1951). Some social and psychological consequences of the longwall method of coal-getting. Human Relations, 4(1), 3–38. 

Trist, E. L. (1981). The evolution of socio-technical systems: A conceptual framework and an action research program (Occasional Paper No. 2). Ontario Quality of Working Life Centre. 

U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2025, August 28). Data scientists. In Occupational outlook handbook. U.S. Department of Labor. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/math/data-scientists.htm 

Wenger, E. (1998). Communities of practice: Learning, meaning, and identity. Cambridge University Press.

Yan, W., Lowell, V. L., & Yang, L. (2024). Developing English language learners' speaking skills through applying a situated learning approach in VR-enhanced learning experiences. Virtual Reality, 28(4), Article 167. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10055-024-01061-5
